Name: rate_distortion_pruning_optimization
Title: Optimizing Pruning for Model Compression via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating model pruning as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive optimal pruning strategies that minimize task-relevant distortion while achieving high compression rates. This approach will provide a principled way to balance compression and performance, leading to more efficient and effective models.
Related Work: Recent works such as Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019) have explored the theoretical foundations of model compression using rate-distortion theory. However, these works primarily focus on characterizing the trade-offs and theoretical limits. Our proposal aims to go further by developing practical pruning algorithms that are derived from these theoretical formulations, ensuring that the algorithms are not only theoretically sound but also empirically effective.
Abstract: Model compression is a critical technique for reducing the computational and memory requirements of deep neural networks. We propose a novel approach to model pruning by framing it as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the distortion (loss in task performance) while achieving a high compression rate. Using rate-distortion theory, we derive optimal pruning strategies that balance compression and performance. Our approach involves an information-theoretic distortion measure that quantifies the impact of pruning on the model's output. We validate our method on several benchmarks, demonstrating that it outperforms existing pruning techniques in terms of compression rate and task performance.

Experiments.Description: Derive the rate-distortion function for model pruning using a task-relevant distortion measure such as cross-entropy loss.
Experiments.Method: Formulate the pruning problem as a constrained optimization problem where the objective is to minimize distortion subject to a rate constraint. Use Lagrange multipliers to derive the rate-distortion function.
Experiments.Metrics: Evaluate the rate-distortion function to ensure it provides a meaningful trade-off between compression rate and task performance.

Experiments.Description: Develop a pruning algorithm based on the derived rate-distortion function.
Experiments.Method: Implement a pruning algorithm that iteratively prunes weights while monitoring the distortion. Use gradient-based methods to optimize the pruning process.
Experiments.Metrics: Measure the compression rate and task performance (e.g., accuracy, F1-score) on benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.

Experiments.Description: Compare the proposed pruning algorithm with existing state-of-the-art pruning methods.
Experiments.Method: Conduct ablation studies to compare the performance of our algorithm with methods like L1-norm pruning, magnitude-based pruning, and lottery ticket hypothesis-based pruning.
Experiments.Metrics: Use metrics such as compression rate, task performance, and computational efficiency to evaluate the comparative performance.

Experiments.Description: Analyze the sensitivity of the pruning algorithm to different distortion measures.
Experiments.Method: Experiment with different distortion measures, such as mean squared error, Kullback-Leibler divergence, and cross-entropy loss, to understand their impact on the pruning process.
Experiments.Metrics: Evaluate the robustness and effectiveness of the algorithm under different distortion measures.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The derived rate-distortion function may be complex and computationally expensive to compute, especially for large models.
The choice of distortion measure can significantly impact the performance of the pruning algorithm, and finding the optimal measure might require extensive experimentation.
The theoretical bounds derived from rate-distortion theory might not always translate perfectly to practical settings, and empirical validation is essential.
The proposed method assumes that the model's performance can be accurately quantified by the chosen distortion measure, which might not always be the case in real-world scenarios.
